title: BSN Registration in the Netherlands
metaTitle: BSN Registration in the Netherlands: How Expats Get a BSN
subtitle: How expats receive a BSN (citizen service number) when registering with a Dutch municipality.
description: Learn how BSN registration works in the Netherlands. Understand when expats receive a citizen service number, which documents may be requested, and what to arrange next.

quickAnswers.label: What is a BSN?
quickAnswers.value: A personal citizen service number used by Dutch government systems.

quickAnswers.label: When do you receive it?
quickAnswers.value: Usually during municipality registration after arrival.

quickAnswers.label: Why it matters
quickAnswers.value: You need it for salary payments, banking, health insurance and DigiD.

sections.heading: Overview
body: A BSN (burgerservicenummer) is the personal identification number used by Dutch government systems. Most expats receive their BSN when they register their address with a municipality after arriving in the Netherlands. This guide explains how BSN registration works, which documents may be requested, and what people usually arrange next.

You do not usually apply separately for a BSN; it is created when you register with your municipality. Timing and document requirements vary by municipality and situation. Receiving a BSN unlocks many next steps, from opening a bank account to arranging health insurance and applying for DigiD.
bullets: Register your address at the municipality (gemeente)
Attend your municipality appointment
Receive your BSN
Use your BSN for banking, insurance, work, and DigiD

sections.heading: Why the BSN is important
body: The BSN (burgerservicenummer) is a personal identification number used across government and administrative systems in the Netherlands. Many everyday services rely on your BSN to connect your identity with tax, healthcare, and employment records.
bullets: Employment contracts and salary processing typically require your BSN
Health insurance registration uses your BSN
Dutch bank account opening often requires your BSN
DigiD setup is linked to your BSN
The Dutch tax administration identifies you using your BSN
Government systems and records use your BSN for official correspondence

links.label: Open a bank account in the Netherlands

sections.heading: How you usually receive your BSN
body: In most situations you do not apply separately for a BSN. The number is created as part of municipality address registration. You schedule an appointment, register your residential address, and the municipality processes the registration and confirms your BSN.
bullets: Arrive in the Netherlands
Schedule a municipality registration appointment
Register your residential address
The municipality processes your registration
Your BSN is assigned and confirmed

personaExample.title: Example: expat arriving for work
personaExample.text: An expat moving to Amsterdam for work typically schedules a municipality appointment shortly after arrival. During the appointment they register their address and receive their BSN, which then allows them to open a bank account and finalize health insurance.

links.label: Register your address in the Netherlands

sections.heading: BSN registration timeline
body: A typical flow for most expats helps you see when and how BSN registration fits into the arrival sequence. Many expats complete these steps within their first days or weeks after arrival. Busy municipalities may have longer waits for appointments; registration timing can affect when you can arrange banking, insurance, and other admin.
bullets: Arrive in the Netherlands
Secure temporary or permanent housing
Schedule your municipality registration appointment
Register your address at the appointment
Receive your BSN during or after registration

sections.heading: Documents often requested during registration
body: Municipalities may request different documents depending on your situation and immigration status. Document requirements vary by municipality and by your immigration and household situation. Preparing documents ahead of time can help avoid delays during your appointment.
bullets: Passport or identity document
Residence permit (if applicable)
Rental agreement or housing confirmation
Birth certificate (sometimes requested)
Marriage certificate when registering with a partner (if applicable)
Supporting documents as requested by your municipality

image.alt: Infographic: documents often requested for BSN registration in the Netherlands—passport, residence permit, rental agreement, birth certificate, marriage certificate, and supporting documents from the municipality

links.label: Documents needed to move to the Netherlands

sections.heading: Where you register for a BSN
body: You register at the municipality (gemeente) where you live. Most municipalities require an appointment; the exact booking process and document requirements can vary. Larger cities such as Amsterdam, Rotterdam, Utrecht, The Hague, and Eindhoven are common among expats and may have longer wait times for slots.

callout.title: Confirm with your municipality
callout.text: Always confirm the latest appointment and document requirements on your municipality's official website.

links.label: Register your address in the Netherlands

sections.heading: When expats usually schedule their appointment
body: Many expats schedule their municipality appointment shortly after arrival. Some municipalities allow booking in advance if you already know your address. Availability may vary in large cities, and your registration timing often influences when you can arrange banking and insurance.
bullets: Some cities require or prefer online appointments
Availability may vary depending on demand
Temporary housing may not always support registration—check with your municipality
Registration timing can influence how quickly you can complete other admin

callout.title: What tends to take longer than expected
callout.text: Limited appointment availability in busy periods, temporary housing that may not support registration, and busy relocation seasons can slow scheduling. As soon as you know the address you will register at, check municipality appointment availability.

sections.heading: What expats typically arrange next
body: Once you receive your BSN, many practical setup tasks become easier to complete because your identity is now registered in Dutch administrative systems. Expats often use this moment to finish setting up the essentials for daily life.
bullets: Opening a Dutch bank account
Arranging health insurance
Activating a mobile phone plan
Setting up DigiD
Planning first-90-days admin and follow-up tasks

faq.q: How long does it take to receive a BSN?
faq.a: Most expats receive their BSN during or shortly after the municipality registration appointment. Exact timing can vary by municipality and situation.

faq.q: Can you open a bank account without a BSN?
faq.a: Some banks allow temporary setups or specific options for people who do not yet have a BSN, but most standard accounts eventually require one. See our guide on opening a bank account before BSN for details.
Students who register their address in the Netherlands usually receive a BSN as part of that registration.

faq.q: Can you receive a BSN without registering an address?
faq.a: Some temporary options exist for non-residents in specific situations, but most expats receive a BSN when they register their address with a municipality.

faq.q: Do all municipalities ask for the same documents?
faq.a: No. Document requirements vary by municipality and by your immigration and household situation. Always check your municipality's official website for the latest list.

faq.q: When should I book my municipality appointment?
faq.a: Many expats book as soon as they know the address they will register at. In busier cities, appointment slots can be limited, so checking availability early is often helpful.
